questions.question: You have 500 labelled images for a new task close to the ImageNet distribution. Which regime makes the most sense?
options: Train a ResNet-50 from scratch
Fine-tune every layer with the same large learning rate
Freeze an ImageNet-pretrained backbone and train only a new linear head
Ignore pretrained weights; small datasets work best with small custom models
questions.explanation: With 500 images you do not have enough signal to train a deep network from scratch or to safely fine-tune all layers end-to-end without destroying pretrained features. Freezing the backbone and training only a new head (linear probe) uses the pretrained features directly and is the standard recipe for small, close-domain datasets.

questions.question: In `make_feature_extractor`, why is `model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, num_classes)` called *after* `for p in model.parameters(): p.requires_grad = False`?
options: PyTorch raises an error if you replace the head before freezing parameters

A newly assigned `nn.Linear` defaults to `requires_grad=True`, so placing the assignment after the freeze leaves only the new head trainable — if the order were reversed, the head would also be frozen and you would have to manually unfreeze it
Freezing after head replacement destroys the backbone's pretrained weights
PyTorch's optimizer ignores parameters added after the freeze call
questions.explanation: When you do `for p in model.parameters(): p.requires_grad = False`, that loop covers all parameters that exist at that moment. A subsequent `model.fc = nn.Linear(...)` creates a fresh module whose parameters default to requires_grad=True. This ordering ensures backbone is frozen and the new head is the only thing the optimizer updates.

questions.question: In `discriminative_param_groups(model, base_lr=1e-3, decay=0.3)` with 6 stages (indices 0–5), each stage's LR is `base_lr * decay^(5 - i)`. The stem (`conv1+bn1`) is at index 0. What LR does the stem receive?
options: 1e-3 — the stem gets the full base LR
3e-4 — one decay step below the head
2.43e-5 — four decay steps below the head
2.43e-6 — five decay steps: 1e-3 × 0.3^5 ≈ 2.43e-6, making the stem train at about 0.24% of the head's LR
questions.explanation: With decay=0.3 and 5 steps below the head: 1e-3 × 0.3^5 = 1e-3 × 0.00243 ≈ 2.43e-6. The stem contains generic edge detectors that should barely move; the extreme ratio (0.24%) is deliberate. Empirically, this prevents catastrophic forgetting of early features while still allowing the backbone to adapt.

questions.question: The `freeze_bn_stats` helper calls `m.eval()` on every BatchNorm module inside the training loop. Why must it be called at the start of each epoch, immediately after `model.train()`?
options: `model.train()` switches all modules — including BN — to training mode, which would update ImageNet running statistics using your small target dataset and corrupt them; `freeze_bn_stats` reverts only BN layers to eval mode so the running buffers stay fixed
`model.train()` deletes BN running statistics; `freeze_bn_stats` reloads them from a checkpoint file
BN layers in eval mode run significantly faster, so calling this speeds up training
BN running stats must be frozen only during the first epoch; subsequent calls have no effect
questions.explanation: model.train() resets every module's training flag, including BatchNorm. In training mode, BN updates its running_mean and running_var with exponential moving averages of the current batch statistics. On a small or distribution-shifted dataset those buffers would drift away from their ImageNet values, hurting every layer that follows. freeze_bn_stats reverses only the BN flip without touching the rest of the model.

questions.question: When fine-tuning end-to-end with discriminative learning rates, why should early layers get a smaller LR than late layers?
options: Early layers encode general visual features you want to preserve; late layers encode task-specific features that must adapt; a smaller LR on early layers prevents feature drift and catastrophic forgetting
Early layers have fewer parameters so they need less gradient signal
PyTorch requires layer-wise LRs for numerical correctness
A smaller LR in early layers speeds up training by reducing the number of optimizer steps
questions.explanation: Early layers already encode the right visual primitives (edges, textures, gradients); you want tiny updates so those features are preserved. Late layers need to move toward the new task. One learning rate for the whole model forces a compromise that hurts both ends. Discriminative LRs let each stage move at its own pace, which is the empirical sweet spot for fine-tuning.

questions.question: You compare two runs: (a) linear probe on a frozen ImageNet backbone, 82% accuracy; (b) end-to-end fine-tune on the same data, 78% accuracy. What should you conclude?
options: Fine-tuning is not helpful; ship the linear probe
The dataset is too large for transfer learning

A fine-tune that ends below the linear probe is almost always a training bug — LR too high on the backbone, BN mishandled, or scheduler misconfigured; diagnose before concluding anything about whether transfer learning works
The pretrained weights are poor quality and should be replaced
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning should always beat or match the linear probe, since the linear probe is a special case of fine-tuning with backbone LR=0. If fine-tune is worse, the pipeline is actively destroying pretrained features. The fix is to lower backbone LR, apply discriminative LRs, or freeze BN — not to abandon fine-tuning. Printing the linear-probe baseline first is the standard sanity check before any end-to-end fine-tune.
